era_context: The American Revolutionary War is in its fifth year, with the conflict expanding to the Caribbean and India. The British Empire faces unprecedented simultaneous military challenges across multiple continents. In Europe, enlightened absolutism is at its height under rulers like Joseph II and Catherine II. The Industrial Revolution is accelerating in Britain. China under the Qianlong Emperor remains prosperous but faces growing internal pressures.

events.title: Charleston Captured by British Forces
events.region: Charleston, South Carolina, British North America
events.description: British forces under General Henry Clinton capture Charleston, South Carolina, on 12 May 1780 after a six-week siege. The fall of Charleston represents the largest American military defeat of the Revolutionary War, with approximately 5,400 American troops captured. The British occupation of Charleston and the surrounding Low Country region gives Britain control of the most strategically important southern port and temporarily shifts the war's momentum in Britain's favor. However, the harsh British occupation policies and the subsequent Camden campaign alienate the local population and ultimately contribute to renewed American resistance.

sources.name: British military dispatches and official reports (1780)
American accounts and contemporary journals
Clinton, Henry. The American Rebellion (memoir, published 1954 but based on contemporary records)

events.certainty_note: The siege and capture of Charleston are extensively documented by both British and American sources; the date is certain.

events.title: Second Mysore War Begins
events.region: Kingdom of Mysore and Madras Presidency, India
events.description: The Second Mysore War (1780–1784) begins when Hyder Ali of Mysore, allied with the French, attacks the British East India Company's territories in southern India. The war is fought across the Deccan and Carnatic regions and involves naval battles in the Indian Ocean. The conflict is part of the broader Anglo-French competition for dominance in India and represents a serious challenge to British expansion. Though the British ultimately prevail, the war is costly and demonstrates the military capability of Indian states when well-organized and equipped with modern weapons.

sources.name: British East India Company records and military dispatches
French naval and military records
Orme, Robert. A History of the Military Transactions of the British Nation in Indostan

events.certainty_note: The war's beginning date and participants are well-documented in British and French military records.

events.title: Battle of Camden
events.region: Camden, South Carolina, British North America
events.description: On 16 August 1780, British forces under General Cornwallis decisively defeat American forces under General Horatio Gates at the Battle of Camden, South Carolina. The American army is routed, with approximately 1,000 killed or wounded and 1,000 captured. Camden is one of the most lopsided American defeats of the Revolutionary War and temporarily establishes British military dominance in the southern theater. However, the victory proves hollow as the harsh British occupation policies and the subsequent rise of American irregular forces under leaders like Francis Marion ('the Swamp Fox') undermine British control.

sources.name: British military dispatches and Cornwallis's reports
American eyewitness accounts and journals
Tarleton, Banastre. A History of the Campaigns of 1780 and 1781 in the Southern Provinces of North America (1787)

events.certainty_note: The battle's date, location, and outcome are extensively documented; the date is certain.

events.title: Gordon Riots in London
events.description: The Gordon Riots (2–9 June 1780) erupt in London as a violent anti-Catholic protest led by Lord George Gordon, a Protestant activist. The riots are ostensibly triggered by the Catholic Relief Act of 1778, which relaxed some restrictions on Catholics, but reflect deeper social tensions including economic hardship, resentment of the American War, and religious sectarianism. The riots result in approximately 300 deaths, the burning of Catholic chapels and homes, and the temporary breakdown of civil order. The riots are suppressed by military force and result in the execution of several rioters. The event demonstrates the volatility of 18th-century urban society and the persistence of religious tensions in Britain.
key_figures: King George III (who authorized military response)
Lord George Germain (Secretary of State for America)

sources.name: Contemporary newspaper accounts (London newspapers, June 1780)
Government and military dispatches
Dickens, Charles. Barnaby Rudge (1841, historical novel based on contemporary accounts)

events.certainty_note: The riots' dates, scale, and causes are extensively documented in contemporary sources.

events.title: Túpac Amaru II Rebellion Suppressed in Peru
events.description: The Túpac Amaru II Rebellion, which began in 1780 in the Peruvian highlands, is brutally suppressed by Spanish colonial forces by the end of 1781. The rebellion, led by José Gabriel Condorcanqui (who claimed descent from the Inca royal line and took the name Túpac Amaru II), mobilizes tens of thousands of indigenous Quechua and Aymara peoples against Spanish colonial rule and the exploitative encomienda and mita systems. Though ultimately defeated, the rebellion represents the largest indigenous uprising in colonial South America and forces the Spanish crown to implement some administrative reforms. Túpac Amaru II is captured and executed in Cuzco on 18 May 1781.
key_figures: Túpac Amaru II (José Gabriel Condorcanqui, rebel leader)

sources.name: Spanish colonial administrative records and military dispatches
Indigenous oral traditions and later compilations
Lewin, Boleslao. La rebelión de Túpac Amaru (1957, based on colonial archives)

events.certainty_note: The rebellion's occurrence and suppression are documented in Spanish colonial records. Túpac Amaru II's execution date is 18 May 1781.

events.title: Serfdom Abolished in Bohemia (Joseph II)
events.description: Emperor Joseph II of Austria issues decrees in 1780 that begin the process of abolishing serfdom in Bohemia and other Austrian territories. The decrees grant peasants personal freedom and the right to leave the land, though they remain subject to certain obligations and taxes. Joseph II's reforms, part of his 'enlightened absolutism,' represent a significant shift in peasant rights and foreshadow the broader abolition of serfdom across Central Europe in the 19th century. The reforms are implemented unevenly and face resistance from the nobility, but they establish the principle that serfdom is incompatible with enlightened governance.
key_figures: Joseph II (Holy Roman Emperor and Austrian ruler)

sources.name: Austrian imperial decrees and official publications (1780)
Contemporary correspondence of Joseph II

events.certainty_note: The decrees' issuance is documented, though the exact date and scope of implementation varied by region. 1780 is the conventional date for the beginning of Joseph II's serfdom reforms.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor Completes the Ten Great Campaigns
events.region: Qing Dynasty, China and Central Asia
events.description: The Qianlong Emperor of the Qing Dynasty completes his series of Ten Great Campaigns (十大武功) by 1780, which have expanded Qing territorial control to its maximum extent. The campaigns, conducted between 1755 and 1780, include the conquest of Xinjiang (1755–1759), the subjugation of Tibet (1750–1792), and campaigns against the Dzungar Khanate and other Central Asian powers. By 1780, the Qing Empire reaches its territorial apex, controlling approximately 13 million square kilometers. These campaigns establish Qing dominance over Central Asia and secure the empire's western frontier, though they also strain the imperial treasury and military resources.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and military chronicles (Qing Shilu)
Jesuit missionary accounts from the Qing court
Spence, Jonathan D. The Search for Modern China (1990, historiographic synthesis)

events.certainty_note: The completion of the Ten Great Campaigns by 1780 is documented in Qing imperial records. The date 1780 is conventional for the end of this military expansion phase.

events.title: Marquis de Lafayette Arrives in America
events.description: The Marquis de Lafayette, a French aristocrat and military officer, arrives in Boston in April 1780 to rejoin the American Revolutionary cause. Lafayette had previously served without pay as a major general in the Continental Army (1777–1779) and had returned to France to advocate for increased French support. His return in 1780 with promises of additional French military aid and reinforcements significantly boosts American morale and military capability. Lafayette plays a crucial role in coordinating American and French forces, particularly in the Yorktown campaign of 1781.

sources.name: Lafayette's correspondence and memoirs
American military records and Washington's correspondence
Gottschalk, Louis R. Lafayette Joins the American Army (1937, based on primary sources)

events.certainty_note: Lafayette's arrival in Boston in April 1780 is well-documented in contemporary correspondence and records.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the British victories at Charleston and Camden in 1780 is often overstated in narratives that emphasize British military superiority. While tactically successful, these victories did not translate into strategic advantage because: (1) the harsh occupation policies alienated the local population, (2) American irregular forces (particularly Francis Marion's 'Swamp Fox' militia) continued to harass British forces, and (3) the victories consumed resources that Britain needed elsewhere. Some historians argue that the British victories in the South actually prolonged the war by creating a false sense of progress, delaying the political negotiations that might have ended the conflict earlier. Additionally, the Túpac Amaru II Rebellion is sometimes portrayed as a purely indigenous uprising, but recent scholarship emphasizes the participation of mestizos and even some creoles, complicating the narrative of a simple indigenous-versus-Spanish conflict.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1780 is exceptionally rich for European and North American military and political events, particularly those involving Britain, France, and the American colonies. The record is less detailed for Asia and Africa, though British East India Company records provide substantial documentation of the Second Mysore War. The Túpac Amaru II Rebellion is documented primarily through Spanish colonial administrative records, which may overemphasize Spanish perspectives and underrepresent indigenous viewpoints. The Qianlong Emperor's Ten Great Campaigns are well-documented in Qing imperial records but are less familiar to Western historiography. The Gordon Riots are extensively documented in contemporary British sources but are sometimes overlooked in broader historical narratives focused on the American Revolution.

graph_edges.note: The capture of Charleston enabled the British to launch the Camden campaign
The Camden victory emboldened Cornwallis to advance into North Carolina and Virginia, leading to Yorktown
The Second Mysore War continued until the Treaty of Mangalore in 1784
The Túpac Amaru II Rebellion was suppressed in 1781, the same year as Yorktown
Joseph II's 1780 serfdom decrees were followed by the more comprehensive Serfdom Patent of 1781
Lafayette's return with French reinforcements enabled the Yorktown campaign
